fields.title: Cugnot's Steam-Powered Vehicle
fields.content: Nicolas-Joseph Cugnot invented the first self-propelled vehicle in 1769. It used steam power for locomotion and found military application in the French army. However, Cugnot's automobile was never commercially sold.

fields.title: Era of Steam-Powered Vehicles
fields.content: In the beginning, the automobile industry was dominated by steam-powered vehicles. These vehicles were expensive, difficult to maintain, and prone to boiler explosions, which deterred potential purchasers.

fields.title: Karl Benz and the First Gasoline-Powered Vehicle
fields.content: The commercial history of automobiles started with the invention of gasoline-powered internal combustion engines. German inventor Karl Benz constructed his first gasoline-powered vehicle in 1885 at Mannheim, Germany.

fields.title: Commercial Production of Benz Cars
fields.content: Commercial production of Benz cars started in 1888, marking a significant milestone in automotive history.

fields.title: Panhard et Levassor: First Exclusive Car Manufacturer
fields.content: Panhard et Levassor of France was the first company to exclusively build and sell motor cars, starting in 1889.

fields.title: Rise of Automobile Manufacturing Companies
fields.content: The early 1900s saw many automobile manufacturing companies coming into existence in various European countries and the United States.

fields.title: The Curved-Dash Oldsmobile
fields.content: The first mass-produced automobile in the United States was the curved-dash Oldsmobile. It was a three-horsepower machine and sold 5,000 units by 1904.

fields.title: Henry Ford and the Model T
fields.content: The economics of the US car market was disrupted by the arrival of Henry Ford and his Model T car. The Model T was the world's first mass-produced vehicle, with a million units sold by 1920, just 10 years after its introduction.

fields.title: The Assembly Line Revolution
fields.content: In 1913, Henry Ford introduced the moving assembly line, revolutionizing automobile production and significantly reducing manufacturing time and costs.

fields.title: The Rise of General Motors
fields.content: General Motors was founded in 1908 by William C. Durant. It grew to become one of the largest automobile manufacturers in the world.

fields.title: The First Electric Starter
fields.content: Charles Kettering invented the electric starter in 1911, eliminating the need for hand-cranking and making cars much easier to start.

fields.title: The Advent of Four-Wheel Brakes
fields.content: In the 1920s, four-wheel brake systems were introduced, significantly improving vehicle safety.

fields.title: The First Automatic Transmission
fields.content: General Motors introduced the first automatic transmission, called the Hydra-Matic, in 1940.

fields.title: The Volkswagen Beetle
fields.content: The Volkswagen Beetle, designed in the 1930s, became one of the best-selling cars of all time, with production continuing until 2003.

fields.title: The Rise of Japanese Automakers
fields.content: In the 1960s and 1970s, Japanese automakers like Toyota and Honda began to gain significant market share in the global automotive industry.

fields.title: The Oil Crisis and Fuel Efficiency
fields.content: The 1973 oil crisis led to a greater emphasis on fuel efficiency in automobile design and production.

fields.title: The Introduction of Airbags
fields.content: Airbags were first introduced in the 1970s and became standard safety features in most vehicles by the 1990s.

fields.title: The First Hybrid Car
fields.content: The Toyota Prius, launched in 1997, was the first mass-produced hybrid electric vehicle.

fields.title: The Rise of Tesla and Electric Vehicles
fields.content: Tesla Motors, founded in 2003, played a significant role in popularizing electric vehicles in the 21st century.

fields.title: The Development of Self-Driving Cars
fields.content: In the 2010s, major automakers and tech companies began developing and testing self-driving car technologies.

fields.title: The Diesel Engine
fields.content: Rudolf Diesel invented the diesel engine in 1893, which later became widely used in trucks and some passenger vehicles.

fields.title: The First Car Radio
fields.content: The first car radio was introduced in the 1930s, changing the driving experience forever.

fields.title: The Introduction of Power Steering
fields.content: Power steering was first introduced in the 1951 Chrysler Imperial, making driving easier and more comfortable.

fields.title: The Rotary Engine
fields.content: Mazda popularized the rotary engine in cars with its RX series, starting in the 1960s.

fields.title: The Rise of SUVs
fields.content: Sport Utility Vehicles (SUVs) gained immense popularity in the 1990s and 2000s, changing the landscape of the automotive market.

fields.title: The Development of Crash Tests
fields.content: Crash testing became standardized in the 1970s, leading to significant improvements in vehicle safety.

fields.title: The First Turbocharged Production Car
fields.content: The Oldsmobile Jetfire and the Chevrolet Corvair Monza were the first turbocharged production cars, both introduced in 1962.

fields.title: The Introduction of Anti-lock Braking Systems
fields.content: Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS) were first introduced in production cars in the 1970s, significantly improving vehicle safety.

fields.title: The First GPS Navigation System in Cars
fields.content: The first GPS navigation system for automobiles was introduced in the 1990s, revolutionizing how people navigate while driving.

fields.title: The Development of Catalytic Converters
fields.content: Catalytic converters were introduced in the 1970s to reduce harmful emissions from automobiles.
